Q1: I kept my study routine pretty flexible, honestly. I'd usually carve out about an hour a day, mostly listening to lecture recordings while I was doing other things like cooking or commuting. I'm an auditory learner, so I found that playing things back multiple times helped more than sitting and reading. I didn't have a strict schedule written down, but I knew roughly when I'd fit studying in each week.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. Some weeks I'd be pretty consistent, maybe studying a bit each evening, but when deadlines approached I'd cluster my effort more intensely. I tried to stay ahead, but life has a way of throwing curveballs, so I adapted as needed. The regularity came more from habit than from a rigid timetable.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was new content posted or when an assignment deadline was coming up. Sometimes I'd get a notification and that would prompt me to check in. Other times, I'd just remember during the day that I should see if there were any announcements or discussion posts I'd missed. It wasn't always scheduled—more reactive, I suppose.
Q4: There were stretches where logging in became almost automatic, especially mid-semester when the gamified tasks were rolling out. I'd check in to see my progress on those challenges, which made it feel less like a chore. Once I got into that rhythm, it stuck for a while. But I wouldn't say it was ever deeply ingrained as a daily habit—more like a regular check-in a few times a week.
Q5: The recorded lectures and audio materials were gold for me. I could listen to them repeatedly, pause, rewind, and really absorb the concepts. The discussion forums were also helpful because hearing other students' perspectives—or reading them, I guess—gave me different angles on the material. Those two resources aligned best with how I learn.
Q6: I often skipped over the dense reading PDFs and some of the supplementary text-heavy resources. They just didn't stick for me the way audio did, so I'd skim them at best. I also didn't engage much with some of the optional quizzes if they felt redundant. It wasn't that they were bad—just not how my brain prefers to process information.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it helped me understand something I was struggling with or if it was directly connected to an assessment. The gamified tasks were great because they gave me immediate feedback and a sense of accomplishment. If something felt like busywork or didn't clearly connect to my grades or understanding, I'd deprioritize it.
Q8: Optional activities felt lighter, less stressful, but also easier to skip when I was pressed for time. Required ones had a sense of urgency that kept me accountable. With optional stuff, I'd only engage if I was genuinely curious or had spare time, which wasn't often. The gamified elements blurred that line a bit, making optional things more appealing.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone offline. I also made voice memos summarizing key points and played those back during walks. My notes were handwritten, and I'd review them without ever touching the LMS. A lot of my studying happened completely outside the platform.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or listening to downloaded recordings, and none of that would show up anywhere online. I'd also have study sessions with a friend where we'd talk through concepts verbally—again, invisible to the system. My best learning often happened away from the screen.
Q11: Yes, definitely. I'd watch or listen to something once on the LMS to make sure I had it downloaded correctly, then I'd engage with it multiple times offline. The initial access was just to grab the file. My real studying happened later, on my own terms, usually with headphones in while doing something else.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really stressful. I had a lot going on personally, and even though my anxiety wasn't overwhelming, the pressure built up. There were a couple of weeks where I felt disconnected from the course, just going through the motions. The gamified tasks actually helped pull me back in during that slump.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or juggling multiple deadlines across courses, my LMS usage dropped. I'd do the bare minimum—check announcements, submit what was due—but I wouldn't explore or engage deeply. Fatigue made it hard to focus on anything beyond the essentials, so my online activity looked sparse even when I was still learning offline.
Q14: There were times I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. I'd have the video playing but my mind would be elsewhere, thinking about other responsibilities or just zoning out. It looked like engagement on paper, but I wasn't truly present. Those sessions felt unproductive, and I'd have to revisit the content later.
Q15: I procrastinated on some of the written assignments, even knowing they mattered. Writing isn't my strength, and I'd put it off in favor of listening to lectures or doing the gamified tasks, which felt more manageable. The avoidance wasn't logical, but the stress of writing made me delay until the pressure became unavoidable.
Q16: Deadlines were my main driver, honestly. I'd ramp up my LMS activity significantly in the days before something was due. Without a deadline looming, I'd coast a bit more. It's not ideal, but that external pressure kept me accountable. The gamified elements helped spread things out a little, but deadlines still ruled my schedule.
Q17: Feedback motivated me when it was specific and constructive. If I got a good score or helpful comments, I'd feel energized to keep going. Vague or delayed feedback was less impactful—I'd already moved on mentally. The instant feedback from gamified tasks was particularly effective at keeping me engaged in the moment.
Q18: The course structure gave me enough flexibility to work at my own pace, which I appreciated. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me plan, even if I didn't always follow through perfectly. The gamified components added a layer of structure that made engagement feel less like a burden and more like a challenge to complete.
Q19: The audio and video options made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. What made it harder was when materials were only available as long text documents with no alternative format. Also, when too many things were due at once, it became overwhelming. Spacing out deadlines and offering varied content formats helped a lot.
Q20: I think some students might be exploring, trying to understand what's expected, or looking for resources without actually completing tasks. Maybe they're anxious about starting or unsure how to approach the work. High activity could also mean they're struggling and searching for help repeatedly without finding what they need.
Q21: That resonates with me. I performed well but my LMS activity was relatively low because I did so much offline. Listening to downloaded lectures, reviewing handwritten notes, discussing with peers—none of that shows up in the system. Low activity doesn't necessarily mean low effort or poor understanding.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like login frequency, submission times, and which resources were accessed. But they miss the depth of engagement—whether someone actually understood the material or just clicked through. They also miss all offline studying, peer discussions, and the emotional or cognitive effort behind the numbers.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively trying to understand, not just completing tasks mechanically. It's when I'm curious, asking questions in my head, connecting ideas to what I already know. Engagement is about quality of attention, not just showing up or logging in.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned through listening offline, the conversations I had with classmates, or the personal notes I made. It also wouldn't capture the stress I managed or how the gamified tasks genuinely lifted my mood and kept me coming back. My real engagement story lives mostly outside the data.
